Let's talk about the Firefox story. 574 points. Fingerprint.com found a stable identifier that links all your Tor identities — and it survives the New Identity button.
A

This one is structurally important. The IndexedDB insertion order in Firefox is deterministic. A website creates databases, reads the ordering, gets a fingerprint. Same identifier cross-origin. Your Tor identity is not reset because the in-process state persists.
B
That's the whole point of Tor Browser. If New Identity doesn't actually reset your fingerprint surface, you have a false privacy guarantee. What's the fix?
A

Canonicalize the output before returning it. Simple. But it's an example of a category — every deterministic property of a runtime is a potential fingerprint vector if it's observable cross-origin. This is the attack class, not just the bug.
B

Moving to the Apple story — 507 points. iOS 26.4.2 patches a bug where deleted Signal messages were sitting in the notification cache for up to a month. The FBI used this to recover messages from a suspect.
A

Here's what this tells us. Signal did everything right at the application layer. Messages deleted, ephemeral timers enforced. But the OS notification system was keeping a plaintext copy independently. That's a second store — a parallel copy outside your deletion scope.
B
So the lesson for any app developer is: if your notification delivery path shows message content, the OS owns a copy of that content. Forever, unless you explicitly audit that path.
A
And Apple patched outside the normal update cycle, which signals this was actively being used. The urgency is real.
B
OK, let's talk about the AI coding behavior stories. Over-editing at 336 points, Zed parallel agents at 204. These two together are telling a story.
A

The over-editing research formalizes something practitioners have been seeing for months. Models instructed to fix three lines are rewriting forty. The training signal rewards comprehensive, well-structured code — not minimal, scoped changes. That reward creates the failure mode.
B

And then Zed ships parallel agents on the same day. Multiple agents, same editor window, per-thread model selection, 120fps rendering. The tooling for running more agents faster is here. But if each agent over-edits independently—
A

The review complexity scales superlinearly. One agent making unscoped changes is manageable. Two agents making simultaneous unscoped changes in adjacent code is a merge conflict that compounds. The tooling is ahead of the model behavior.
B
What should a builder do with this right now?
A

Add explicit scope constraints to every agentic task. Not just 'fix this bug' — 'fix only this function, modify no other files.' Until models are trained on minimal-edit signals, the scope constraint is yours to enforce.
